content.text:
*"He must become greater; I must become less."*{John 3:30}{*"The kernel measures what is lost; the image reveals what is preserved."*}{Emil Artin}

*"The kernel is what gets collapsed; the image is what remains."*{— Algebraic principle}

The kernel of a homomorphism tells us what gets "collapsed" to the 
identity, while the image tells us what the homomorphism actually 
produces. Together, they completely describe the homomorphism.

content.title: Kernel
content.content:
The **kernel** of a homomorphism $\phi: G \to H$ is
\[
 \ker(\phi) = \{g \in G : \phi(g) = e_H\}
\]

content.title: Image
content.content:
The **image** of $\phi: G \to H$ is
\[
 \text{Im}(\phi) = \phi(G) = \{\phi(g) : g \in G\}
\]

content.title: Kernel and Image are Subgroups
content.content:
Let $\phi: G \to H$ be a homomorphism. Then:

1. $\ker(\phi) \trianglelefteq G$ (kernel is a normal subgroup of $G$)

2. $\text{Im}(\phi) \leq H$ (image is a subgroup of $H$)
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
**(i)** Let $a, b \in \ker(\phi)$. Then 
$\phi(ab^{-1}) = \phi(a)\phi(b)^{-1} = e_H e_H^{-1} = e_H$, so 
$ab^{-1} \in \ker(\phi)$.

For normality: if $g \in G$ and $n \in \ker(\phi)$, then 
$\phi(gng^{-1}) = \phi(g)\phi(n)\phi(g)^{-1} = \phi(g) e_H \phi(g)^{-1} 
= e_H$.

**(ii)** Let $\phi(a), \phi(b) \in \text{Im}(\phi)$. Then 
$\phi(a)\phi(b)^{-1} = \phi(ab^{-1}) \in \text{Im}(\phi)$.

content.title: Injectivity Criterion
content.content:
A homomorphism $\phi: G \to H$ is injective if and only if 
$\ker(\phi) = \{e_G\}$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
$(\Rightarrow)$ If $\phi$ is injective and $\phi(g) = e_H = \phi(e_G)$, 
then $g = e_G$.

$(\Leftarrow)$ Suppose $\ker(\phi) = \{e_G\}$. If $\phi(a) = \phi(b)$, 
then $\phi(ab^{-1}) = \phi(a)\phi(b)^{-1} = e_H$, so $ab^{-1} \in 
\ker(\phi)$, hence $ab^{-1} = e_G$, giving $a = b$.

content.title: First Isomorphism Theorem (Preview)
content.content:
If $\phi: G \to H$ is a homomorphism, then:
\[
 G/\ker(\phi) \cong \text{Im}(\phi)
\]
In particular, if $G$ is finite, $|G| = |\ker(\phi)| \cdot |\text{Im}(\phi)|$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Application of counting
content.problem:
The homomorphism $\phi: \Z_{12} \to \Z_{12}$ defined by $\phi(x) = 3x$ 
has:

- $\ker(\phi) = \{0, 4, 8\}$ (solutions to $3x \equiv 0 \pmod{12}$)

- $\text{Im}(\phi) = \{0, 3, 6, 9\}$

- Check: $|\ker(\phi)| \cdot |\text{Im}(\phi)| = 3 \cdot 4 = 12 = 
 |\Z_{12}|$.

items:
Find the kernel and image of $\phi: \Z \to \Z$ defined by 
 $\phi(n) = 3n$.

Find the kernel and image of $\phi: D_4 \to \Z_2$ defined by 
 $\phi(\sigma) = 0$ if $\sigma$ is a rotation and $\phi(\sigma) = 1$ 
 if $\sigma$ is a reflection.

Prove that every normal subgroup is the kernel of some 
 homomorphism.

marginNotes.text:
His winnowing fork is in his hand, and 
he will clear his threshing floor, gathering his wheat into the barn.
